news_summary: Today's space news highlights groundbreaking discoveries with a rare supernova offering insights into the Universe’s expansion, inspiring contributions to lunar exploration, and innovative international efforts mapping Earth's changing landscapes from space.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met en avant des découvertes révolutionnaires : une supernova rare qui nous éclaire sur l'expansion de l'Univers, des contributions inspirantes à l'exploration lunaire, ainsi que des initiatives internationales novatrices visant à cartographier depuis l'espace les paysages terrestres en constante évolution.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy destacan descubrimientos revolucionarios, como una supernova poco común que ofrece información sobre la expansión del universo, contribuciones inspiradoras a la exploración lunar e iniciativas internacionales innovadoras para cartografiar desde el espacio los paisajes cambiantes de la Tierra.

articles.title: A one-in-a-million supernova seen five times could reveal the Universe’s true speed
articles.summary: A spectacular cosmic event nicknamed “SN Winny” could help solve one of astronomy’s biggest mysteries: how fast the universe is expanding. This rare superluminous supernova, located 10 billion light-years away, appears five times in the sky thanks to gravitational lensing, creating a dazzling “cosmic fireworks” effect. By measuring the slight delays between each appearance—caused by light taking different paths around two foreground galaxies—scientists can directly calculate the universe’s expansion rate.
articles.content:
Scientists have known for nearly a century that the universe is expanding, but the exact rate of that expansion is still uncertain. This ongoing debate has even raised questions about the standard model of cosmology. Now, researchers from the Technical University of Munich (TUM), Ludwig Maximilians University (LMU), and the Max Planck Institutes MPA and MPE have identified and analyzed an extremely rare type of supernova that could provide a new and independent way to measure how fast the universe is growing.

The object at the center of this discovery is a superluminous supernova located about 10 billion light-years away. It shines much brighter than typical stellar explosions. What makes it especially remarkable is how it appears in the sky. Instead of a single point of light, it shows up five separate times, creating a striking cosmic display caused by gravitational lensing.

As the supernova's light travels toward Earth, it passes by two galaxies in the foreground. Their gravity bends the light and sends it along multiple paths. Because each path is slightly different in length, the light from each image arrives at different times. By carefully measuring these delays, scientists can calculate the current expansion rate of the universe, known as the Hubble constant.

Sherry Suyu, Associate Professor of Observational Cosmology at TUM and Fellow at the Max Planck Institute for Astrophysics, explains: "We nicknamed this supernova SN Winny, inspired by its official designation SN 2025wny. It is an extremely rare event that could play a key role in improving our understanding of the cosmos. The chance of finding a superluminous supernova perfectly aligned with a suitable gravitational lens is lower than one in a million. We spent six years searching for such an event by compiling a list of promising gravitational lenses, and in August 2025, SN Winny matched exactly with one of them."

High-resolution imaging reveals a unique system

Gravitationally lensed supernovae are extremely uncommon, which means only a small number of these measurements have been made so far. Their reliability depends heavily on how accurately scientists can determine the masses of the galaxies bending the light, since those masses control the strength of the lensing effect.

To improve those measurements, researchers from MPE and LMU used the Large Binocular Telescope in Arizona, USA. Equipped with two 8.4-meter mirrors and an adaptive optics system that reduces atmospheric distortion, the telescope produced the first high-resolution color image of this system.

The image shows two lensing galaxies at the center, surrounded by five bluish points of light that represent the supernova's multiple images. This configuration is unusual, since most similar systems produce only two or four images. By analyzing the positions of all five images, Allan Schweinfurth (TUM) and Leon Ecker (LMU), junior members of the team, created the first detailed model of how mass is distributed in the lensing galaxies.

"Until now, most lensed supernovae were magnified by massive galaxy clusters, whose mass distributions are complex and hard to model," says Allan Schweinfurth. "SN Winny, however, is lensed by just two individual galaxies. We find overall smooth and regular light and mass distributions for these galaxies, suggesting that they have not yet collided in the past despite their close apparent proximity. The overall simplicity of the system offers an exciting opportunity to measure the universe's expansion rate with high accuracy."

Two methods, two very different results

Currently, astronomers rely on two main approaches to measure the Hubble constant, but they do not agree with each other. This disagreement is known as the Hubble tension.

One method focuses on nearby galaxies and builds up distance measurements step by step, similar to climbing a ladder. Because each step depends on the previous one, this approach is called the cosmic distance ladder. It uses objects with known brightness to estimate distances, then compares those distances to how fast galaxies are moving away. However, because it involves many calibration steps, small uncertainties can build up and affect the final result.

The second method looks at the early universe by studying the cosmic microwave background, the faint radiation left over from the Big Bang. Using models of how the universe evolved, scientists can calculate the current expansion rate. While this method is very precise, it depends heavily on assumptions about the universe's history, which are still being examined and debated.

A new one-step method to measure the Hubble constant

A third technique is now emerging based on gravitationally lensed supernovae like SN Winny. Stefan Taubenberger, a key member of Professor Suyu's team and lead author of the supernova identification study, explains that measuring the time delays between the multiple images, combined with knowledge of the lensing galaxies' mass, allows scientists to directly determine the Hubble constant: "Unlike the cosmic distance ladder, this is a one-step method, with fewer and completely different sources of systematic uncertainties."

Astronomers around the world are continuing to observe SN Winny using both ground-based and space-based telescopes. These observations are expected to provide important new data that could help resolve the long-standing disagreement over how fast the universe is expanding.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a rare and significant astronomical discovery—a superluminous supernova seen five times due to gravitational lensing—that offers a novel and potentially more accurate method to measure the universe's expansion rate. This breakthrough addresses a major scientific mystery (the Hubble tension) with broad implications for cosmology and our understanding of the universe. The article provides detailed context about the discovery, the methods used, and its potential impact, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with substantial real-world scientific significance.
articles.personality_title: Rare supernova seen five times offers new way to measure universe’s expansion
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have known for nearly 100 years that the universe is expanding, but they still don’t agree exactly how fast it is growing. This has caused a big puzzle in astronomy called the Hubble tension. To solve it, researchers look for new ways to measure the universe’s expansion rate, called the Hubble constant.

**What happened** – A team from several German universities and institutes found a very rare supernova, a huge star explosion, about 10 billion light-years away. This supernova, nicknamed SN Winny, is special because its light appears five times in the sky. This happens because its light passes by two galaxies on the way to Earth, and their gravity bends the light to create multiple images. Scientists measured tiny delays between when each image appeared to calculate the universe’s expansion.

**Impact** – This is the first time a supernova has been seen in five images caused by just two galaxies, making it easier to understand the system. The method used is different from older ways and could provide a clearer, one-step measurement of how fast the universe is growing. This might help solve the disagreement between current methods that give different results.

**What’s next step** – Astronomers are watching SN Winny with powerful telescopes on the ground and in space to collect more data. These observations will improve their measurements and could bring new answers about the universe’s speed of expansion.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A rare supernova appearing five times due to gravity offers a new, simpler way to measure how fast the universe is expanding, helping to solve a major astronomy puzzle.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une supernova rare vue cinq fois offre une nouvelle méthode pour mesurer l’expansion de l’univers
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques savent depuis près de 100 ans que l’univers s’étend, mais ils ne s’accordent pas encore sur la vitesse exacte de cette expansion. Ce désaccord crée un grand mystère en astronomie appelé la tension de Hubble. Pour y répondre, les chercheurs cherchent de nouvelles façons de mesurer le taux d’expansion, appelé la constante de Hubble.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de plusieurs universités et instituts allemands a découvert une supernova très rare, une énorme explosion d’étoile, située à environ 10 milliards d’années-lumière. Cette supernova, surnommée SN Winny, est spéciale car sa lumière apparaît cinq fois dans le ciel. Cela se produit parce que sa lumière passe près de deux galaxies sur le chemin vers la Terre, et leur gravité la dévie, créant plusieurs images. Les scientifiques ont mesuré de petits décalages entre chaque apparition pour calculer l’expansion de l’univers.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois qu’une supernova est vue en cinq images causées par seulement deux galaxies, ce qui facilite la compréhension du système. Cette méthode est différente des anciennes et pourrait fournir une mesure plus claire et directe de la vitesse d’expansion de l’univers. Cela pourrait aider à résoudre le désaccord entre les méthodes actuelles qui donnent des résultats différents.

**Prochaine étape** – Les astronomes observent SN Winny avec des télescopes puissants au sol et dans l’espace pour collecter plus de données. Ces observations amélioreront leurs mesures et pourraient apporter de nouvelles réponses sur la vitesse d’expansion de l’univers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une supernova rare apparaissant cinq fois grâce à la gravité offre une nouvelle méthode plus simple pour mesurer la vitesse d’expansion de l’univers, aidant à résoudre un grand mystère en astronomie.
articles.personality_title_es: Una supernova rara vista cinco veces ofrece nueva forma de medir la expansión del universo
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos saben desde hace casi 100 años que el universo se está expandiendo, pero no se ponen de acuerdo sobre qué tan rápido crece. Esto ha creado un gran misterio en astronomía llamado la tensión de Hubble. Para resolverlo, buscan nuevas formas de medir la tasa de expansión del universo, llamada constante de Hubble.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de varias universidades e institutos alemanes encontró una supernova muy rara, una enorme explosión estelar, a unos 10 mil millones de años luz. Esta supernova, llamada SN Winny, es especial porque su luz aparece cinco veces en el cielo. Esto ocurre porque la luz pasa cerca de dos galaxias en el camino a la Tierra, y la gravedad de esas galaxias dobla la luz para crear varias imágenes. Los científicos midieron pequeños retrasos en la llegada de cada imagen para calcular la expansión del universo.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que una supernova se ve en cinco imágenes causadas por solo dos galaxias, lo que facilita entender el sistema. El método usado es diferente a los anteriores y podría dar una medición más clara y directa de la velocidad de expansión. Esto podría ayudar a resolver el desacuerdo entre los métodos actuales que dan resultados distintos.

**Próximo paso** – Los astrónomos están observando SN Winny con telescopios potentes en la Tierra y en el espacio para obtener más datos. Estas observaciones mejorarán las mediciones y podrían dar nuevas respuestas sobre la velocidad de expansión del universo.

**Resumen en una frase** – Una supernova rara que aparece cinco veces gracias a la gravedad ofrece una forma nueva y más simple de medir qué tan rápido se expande el universo, ayudando a resolver un gran misterio en astronomía.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cosmic scene showing two elegant, softly glowing galaxies close together at the center, gently bending and splitting the light of a radiant, superluminous star into five distinct shimmering blue orbs arranged around them, all set against a deep, serene space background with subtle gradients of natural dark blues and purples.

articles.title: I Am Artemis: Ryan Schulte
articles.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Ryan Schulte, Orion flywheel project manager: As the four Artemis II astronauts traveled on a 694,481-mile journey around the Moon and back, the Orion spacecraft provided them with all the essentials for deep space life, including daily exercise. The crew used an exercise device called the flywheel throughout their mission to maintain their physical and mental health, and Ryan Schulte, Orion […]
articles.content:
Listen to this audio excerpt from Ryan Schulte, Orion flywheel project manager:













0:00 / 0:00 Your browser does not support the audio element.

As the four Artemis II astronauts traveled on a 694,481-mile journey around the Moon and back, the Orion spacecraft provided them with all the essentials for deep space life, including daily exercise. The crew used an exercise device called the flywheel throughout their mission to maintain their physical and mental health, and Ryan Schulte, Orion flywheel project manager, led the team responsible for developing the flywheel for the historic flight.

At NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston, Schulte oversees the team that designed, built, tested, and flew the flywheel used on Artemis II, and currently develops a fleet of more reusable exercise devices for future Artemis missions.

What we’re doing with this exercise device has a direct impact on the crew’s safety, health, and their mission success. I feel lucky to work on hardware that the crew is physically using, interacting with, and benefiting from on a daily basis. Ryan Schulte Orion Flywheel Project Manager

The flywheel is a compact, multi-functional device about the size of a large shoebox that provides the crew with a range of aerobic and resistive workouts without requiring any electrical power from the spacecraft.

“It works kind of like an inertial yo-yo,” said Schulte.

The user can select different gear ratios for different resistance modes, and the flywheel can provide ultimately up to 500 pounds of resistance.

“It’s really all dependent upon how much effort you put in. The crew can do squats, deadlifts, bent rows, high-pulls, curls, heel raises, and aerobic rowing all in one device.”

Developing the flywheel for Orion posed unique challenges for Schulte’s team, ranging from limited space and crew mobility to reducing noise generation for easy crew communication during workouts.

“One of the biggest challenges was trying to fit everything into this compact box, and also to be able to have enough space inside the rest of the capsule for someone to fully stand up and fully extend at high rates of speed and repetitions,” said Schulte.

The team’s successful response to these challenges was displayed during the approximately 10-day Artemis II mission, where the crew members exercised for roughly 30 minutes per day with the flywheel. The sessions helped to counteract both the physical and mental effects induced by a microgravity environment, which on future, longer-duration Artemis missions, will become an increasingly important component for astronauts.

Ryan Schulte, Orion flywheel project manager, demonstrates a rowing exercise on the Orion flywheel in the Exercise Countermeasures Lab at NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston. NASA/Rad Sinyak

“Without Earth’s gravity, the crew’s muscles, bones, and stamina all begin to atrophy, or weaken,” Schulte said. “Exercise will help prevent injury as crews need to perform long lunar spacewalks on the surface or for emergency egress out of the capsule.”

Exercise with the flywheel also supports the crew’s mental health, providing psychological benefits while living in a compact space inside Orion.

“It’s a great form of stress relief,” Schulte said. “It improves their mental clarity by getting their fluids and their blood flowing, which can stagnate in your head in zero gravity. We’ve talked to some of the crew about how much clearer their minds feel after exercise in flight.”

Schulte began his career as a co-op at Johnson in 2007, then joined NASA full-time as a test engineer for pyrotechnics, propulsion, and power systems. He later transitioned to NASA’s Human Health and Performance Directorate and began working in the Human Research Program, where his interest in human interfaces with engineering grew, eventually leading him to his current role as the flywheel project manager.

With the success of Artemis II and the promise of future missions ahead, Schulte’s work on the flywheel and next generation exercise devices will play a vital role in keeping astronauts safe, healthy, and mission ready on the lunar surface and beyond.
articles.reasoning: The article details a significant technological and health-related advancement in space exploration: the development and successful use of a compact, multifunctional exercise device (the flywheel) on the Artemis II mission. This device supports astronauts' physical and mental health during deep space travel, addressing critical challenges of microgravity. The story highlights a concrete achievement with broad implications for future long-duration space missions, offering tangible benefits for astronaut safety and mission success.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s compact flywheel keeps Artemis II astronauts healthy on moon mission
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artemis II was a mission where four astronauts traveled nearly 700,000 miles around the Moon and back. During this deep space trip, they needed ways to stay physically and mentally healthy despite the challenges of microgravity.

**What happened** – NASA used a special exercise device called the flywheel on the Artemis II spacecraft. Ryan Schulte and his team at Johnson Space Center designed this small, powerful machine to let astronauts do different workouts like squats, rowing, and curls without using electricity. The astronauts exercised about 30 minutes daily during their 10-day mission.

**Impact** – The flywheel helps stop muscles and bones from weakening in space, which happens because of the lack of gravity. It also helps the astronauts feel clearer in their minds by improving blood flow. This compact device fits easily inside the spacecraft and makes almost no noise, so the crew can still talk while exercising. This is important because staying strong and alert is key for astronauts who might have to do long moonwalks or emergency tasks.

**What's next step** – Ryan Schulte’s team is now working on making more reusable exercise devices for future Artemis missions. As missions get longer, these tools will be even more important to keep astronauts safe and ready for their work on the Moon and beyond.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s innovative flywheel exercise device helped Artemis II astronauts stay strong and focused during their trip around the Moon, paving the way for healthier long-term space missions.
articles.personality_title_fr: La roue volante compacte de la NASA maintient la santé des astronautes d’Artemis II lors de la mission lunaire
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Artemis II était une mission où quatre astronautes ont parcouru près de 700 000 miles autour de la Lune et retour. Pendant ce voyage dans l’espace lointain, ils avaient besoin de rester en bonne santé physique et mentale malgré les défis de la microgravité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – La NASA a utilisé un appareil d’exercice spécial appelé la roue volante à bord du vaisseau Orion. Ryan Schulte et son équipe du Centre Spatial Johnson ont conçu cette machine petite mais puissante pour permettre aux astronautes de faire différents exercices comme des squats, de l’aviron et des curls sans utiliser d’électricité. Les astronautes ont fait de l’exercice environ 30 minutes par jour pendant leur mission de 10 jours.

**Impact** – La roue volante aide à empêcher les muscles et les os de s’affaiblir dans l’espace, ce qui arrive à cause de l’absence de gravité. Elle aide aussi les astronautes à avoir un esprit plus clair en améliorant la circulation sanguine. Cet appareil compact s’intègre facilement dans le vaisseau et fait très peu de bruit, ce qui permet à l’équipage de parler pendant l’exercice. Cela est important car rester fort et alerte est essentiel pour les astronautes qui doivent faire de longues sorties sur la Lune ou gérer des urgences.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de Ryan Schulte travaille maintenant à créer plus d’appareils d’exercice réutilisables pour les futures missions Artemis. Comme les missions seront plus longues, ces outils deviendront encore plus indispensables pour garder les astronautes en sécurité et prêts à travailler sur la Lune et au-delà.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le dispositif innovant de roue volante de la NASA a aidé les astronautes d’Artemis II à rester forts et concentrés lors de leur voyage autour de la Lune, ouvrant la voie à des missions spatiales plus longues en meilleure santé.
articles.personality_title_es: La rueda volante compacta de la NASA mantiene saludables a los astronautas de Artemis II en la misión lunar
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Artemis II fue una misión en la que cuatro astronautas viajaron casi 700,000 millas alrededor de la Luna y de regreso. Durante este viaje al espacio profundo, necesitaban mantenerse saludables física y mentalmente a pesar de los desafíos de la microgravedad.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA usó un dispositivo especial de ejercicio llamado rueda volante en la nave Orion. Ryan Schulte y su equipo en el Centro Espacial Johnson diseñaron esta máquina pequeña pero potente para que los astronautas pudieran hacer diferentes ejercicios como sentadillas, remo y curls sin usar electricidad. Los astronautas ejercitaron unos 30 minutos al día durante su misión de 10 días.

**Impacto** – La rueda volante ayuda a evitar que los músculos y huesos se debiliten en el espacio, lo que sucede por la falta de gravedad. También ayuda a los astronautas a tener una mente más clara al mejorar el flujo sanguíneo. Este dispositivo compacto cabe fácilmente en la nave y hace muy poco ruido, por lo que la tripulación puede hablar mientras hace ejercicio. Esto es importante porque mantenerse fuerte y alerta es clave para los astronautas que podrían tener que hacer largas caminatas lunares o tareas de emergencia.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de Ryan Schulte ahora trabaja en crear más dispositivos de ejercicio reutilizables para futuras misiones Artemis. A medida que las misiones sean más largas, estas herramientas serán aún más importantes para mantener a los astronautas seguros y listos para trabajar en la Luna y más allá.

**Resumen en una frase** – El dispositivo innovador de rueda volante de la NASA ayudó a los astronautas de Artemis II a mantenerse fuertes y concentrados durante su viaje alrededor de la Luna, preparando el camino para misiones espaciales más largas y saludables.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a compact, intricately designed flywheel exercise device glowing softly inside a stylized spacecraft cabin, with abstract silhouettes of four astronauts in motion around it, their forms connected by flowing lines symbolizing physical health and mental clarity during a deep space journey around a softly glowing crescent Moon visible through a nearby window.

articles.title: US-Indian Space Mission Maps Extreme Subsidence in Mexico City
articles.summary: One of the most powerful radar systems ever launched into space has mapped the ground moving beneath one of fastest subsiding capitals in the world: Mexico City. The findings show how quickly and reliably the NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite can track real-time changes across Earth’s surface from orbit, unhindered by clouds or vegetation […]
articles.content:
New data from NISAR shows where Mexico City and its environs subsided by up to a few centimeters per month (shown in blue) between Oct. 25, 2025, and Jan. 17, 2026. Uneven and seemingly small elevation changes have added up over the decades, fracturing roads, buildings, and water lines. NASA/JPL-Caltech/David Bekaert

One of the most powerful radar systems ever launched into space has mapped the ground moving beneath one of fastest subsiding capitals in the world: Mexico City. The findings show how quickly and reliably the NISAR (NASA-ISRO Synthetic Aperture Radar) satellite can track real-time changes across Earth’s surface from orbit, unhindered by clouds or vegetation that impede optical sensors and higher-frequency radars.

Home to some 20 million people, the Mexico City area is built atop an aquifer. Extensive groundwater pumping, combined with the weight of urban development, has resulted in the compaction of the ancient lakebed beneath the city for more than a century. An engineer first documented the issue in 1925, and by the 1990s and 2000s, parts of the metropolitan area were sinking by around 14 inches (35 centimeters) per year, damaging infrastructure including the Metro, one of the largest rapid transit systems in the Americas.

Several generations of space-based radar have tracked Mexico City on the move. The NISAR mission, launched in July 2025, is now advancing these efforts, analyzing fast-changing areas that are challenging to survey from space. Capable of working day and night, rain or shine, NISAR’s L-band synthetic aperture radar is designed to track subtle motions such as land sinking and rising, glaciers sliding, and croplands growing, as it passes overhead multiple times a month.

“Images like this confirm that NISAR’s measurements align with expectations,” said Craig Ferguson, deputy project manager at NASA Headquarters in Washington. “NISAR’s long wavelength L-band radar will make it possible to detect and track land subsidence in more challenging and densely vegetated regions such as coastal communities where they may have the compounding effects of both land subsidence and sea level rise.”

The new analysis is based on preliminary measurements taken by NISAR between October 2025 and January 2026, during Mexico City’s dry season. Parts of the region found to be subsiding by more than half an inch (more than 2 centimeters) per month are shown in dark blue. The yellow and red areas are likely residual noise signals that are expected to decrease as NISAR collects more data. The structure near the center of the image is Benito Juarez International Airport, with Lake Nabor Carrillo visible as a dark green oblong to the northeast.

One area landmark — the Angel of Independence along the Paseo de la Reforma — is a visible indicator of subsidence. Built in 1910 to commemorate 100 years of Mexico’s independence, the towering monument stands 114 feet (36 meters) high and has had 14 steps added to its base as the land around it gradually sinks.

“Mexico City is a well-known hot spot when it comes to subsidence, and images like this are just the beginning for NISAR,” said David Bekaert, a project manager at the Flemish Institute for Technological Research and a member of the NISAR science team. “We’re going to see an influx of new discoveries from all over the world, given the unique sensing capabilities of NISAR and its consistent global coverage.”

A joint mission developed by NASA and the Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO), NISAR launched from Satish Dhawan Space Centre on India’s southeastern coast. Managed by Caltech, NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California leads the United States component of the project and provided the satellite’s L-band SAR and antenna reflector. The spacecraft bus and its S-band SAR were provided by ISRO.

The NISAR satellite is the first to carry two SAR instruments at different wavelengths and is monitoring Earth’s land and ice surfaces twice every 12 days, collecting data using the spacecraft’s giant drum-shaped reflector, which measures 39 feet (12 meters) wide — the largest radar antenna reflector NASA has ever sent into space.

To learn more about NISAR, visit:

https://science.nasa.gov/mission/nisar/

Media Contacts

Andrew Wang / Andrew Good

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

626-379-6874 / 818-393-2433

andrew.wang@jpl.nasa.gov / andrew.c.good@jpl.nasa.gov

Written by Sally Younger

2026-027
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the successful deployment and early data from the NISAR satellite, a joint NASA-ISRO mission, which provides powerful new capabilities to monitor land subsidence and other Earth surface changes globally. This has broad significance for urban planning, infrastructure safety, and environmental monitoring, benefiting large populations such as Mexico City's 20 million residents. The detailed description of the satellite's technology and its unique dual-band radar instruments adds substance and context, highlighting a major advancement in Earth observation from space.
articles.personality_title: New Space Radar Maps Ground Sinking in Mexico City with High Precision
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mexico City is one of the fastest sinking cities in the world. Built on an old lakebed, the city’s ground has been slowly sinking for over a century because of heavy water pumping and the weight of buildings.

**What happened** – A new satellite called NISAR, launched in July 2025 by NASA and the Indian Space Research Organisation, has used powerful radar technology to map how much the ground under Mexico City is sinking. Between October 2025 and January 2026, NISAR detected parts of the city sinking by up to a few centimeters every month. This satellite can see through clouds and vegetation, tracking changes day and night.

**Impact** – This radar data shows clearly where the land is moving, helping scientists and city planners understand the risks to roads, buildings, and water pipes. For example, the Angel of Independence monument has needed extra steps added because the ground around it is sinking. NISAR’s ability to measure these changes in real time and in difficult weather makes it a powerful tool for monitoring many places around the world.

**What's next step** – As NISAR collects more data, it will improve our knowledge of land sinking and other changes like glacier movement and farming growth globally. This information can help protect cities and environments by giving early warnings and guiding better planning.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The NISAR satellite is providing detailed, reliable maps of ground sinking in Mexico City, offering new tools to monitor and protect vulnerable urban areas worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau radar spatial cartographie avec précision l'affaissement du sol à Mexico
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Mexico est l'une des villes qui s'enfonce le plus rapidement au monde. Construite sur un ancien lac asséché, la ville s'enfonce lentement depuis plus d'un siècle à cause du pompage intensif d'eau et du poids des constructions.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un nouveau satellite appelé NISAR, lancé en juillet 2025 par la NASA et l'Organisation indienne de recherche spatiale, a utilisé une technologie radar puissante pour cartographier l'affaissement du sol sous Mexico. Entre octobre 2025 et janvier 2026, NISAR a détecté des zones de la ville s'enfonçant de plusieurs centimètres chaque mois. Ce satellite peut voir à travers les nuages et la végétation, surveillant les changements de jour comme de nuit.

**Impact** – Ces données radar montrent clairement où le sol bouge, aidant les scientifiques et les urbanistes à comprendre les risques pour les routes, les bâtiments et les canalisations. Par exemple, le monument de l'Ange de l'Indépendance a dû recevoir des marches supplémentaires car le sol autour s'enfonce. La capacité de NISAR à mesurer ces changements en temps réel et par tous les temps en fait un outil puissant pour surveiller de nombreux endroits dans le monde.

**Prochaine étape** – Au fur et à mesure que NISAR collecte plus de données, il améliorera notre connaissance de l'affaissement des sols et d'autres changements comme le mouvement des glaciers et la croissance des cultures à l'échelle mondiale. Ces informations peuvent aider à protéger les villes et les environnements en fournissant des alertes précoces et en guidant une meilleure planification.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le satellite NISAR fournit des cartes détaillées et fiables de l'affaissement du sol à Mexico, offrant de nouveaux outils pour surveiller et protéger les zones urbaines vulnérables dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo radar espacial mapea con precisión el hundimiento del suelo en Ciudad de México
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Ciudad de México es una de las ciudades que más rápido se hunde en el mundo. Construida sobre un antiguo lago seco, el suelo de la ciudad se ha ido hundiendo lentamente durante más de un siglo debido a la extracción intensiva de agua y al peso de los edificios.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo satélite llamado NISAR, lanzado en julio de 2025 por la NASA y la Organización India de Investigación Espacial, utilizó tecnología radar avanzada para mapear cuánto se está hundiendo el suelo bajo Ciudad de México. Entre octubre de 2025 y enero de 2026, NISAR detectó que partes de la ciudad se hundían varios centímetros cada mes. Este satélite puede ver a través de nubes y vegetación, monitoreando cambios de día y de noche.

**Impacto** – Estos datos radar muestran claramente dónde se mueve la tierra, ayudando a científicos y planificadores a entender los riesgos para carreteras, edificios y tuberías. Por ejemplo, al monumento Ángel de la Independencia le han añadido escalones porque el suelo a su alrededor se hunde. La capacidad de NISAR para medir estos cambios en tiempo real y en cualquier clima lo convierte en una herramienta poderosa para monitorear muchas áreas del mundo.

**Próximo paso** – A medida que NISAR recopile más datos, mejorará nuestro conocimiento sobre el hundimiento de tierras y otros cambios como el movimiento de glaciares y el crecimiento de cultivos a nivel mundial. Esta información puede ayudar a proteger ciudades y ambientes al ofrecer alertas tempranas y guiar una mejor planificación.

**Resumen en una frase** – El satélite NISAR está proporcionando mapas detallados y confiables del hundimiento del suelo en Ciudad de México, ofreciendo nuevas herramientas para monitorear y proteger áreas urbanas vulnerables en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast, gently sinking cityscape symbolized by layered, shifting earth strata beneath iconic abstract shapes representing Mexico City’s landmarks, with a large, elegant radar dish orbiting overhead emitting soft blue waves that ripple downward, illustrating precise satellite tracking of the land’s slow subsidence over time.
